request: a page where the hasse diagram of the sentences is the only navigation: click a node to see its sentence and only the code it owns, everything else folded away

nodes.text: a page ruled by the hasse diagram of the sentences: the diagram and the chosen sentence sit on the left as the only navigation, the code sits on the right, and nothing speaks until a node is clicked. no headers, no toggles, no instructions. the page reads the compiler's json and decides nothing.

clicking a diagram node selects it: the node's sentence appears under the diagram, and the code narrows to what that node owns. clicking the selected node again, or the diagram background, clears the selection. hovering a node previews its lighting without selecting.
cut the code at every range boundary and colour each piece by the deepest fragment that covers it, specified in teal and assumed in ochre
while a node is selected, every span the node does not own collapses into a thin fold that counts the hidden lines, so all the code the node owns is visible at once without scrolling.
read the project list and each project's map and code from disk, normalise line endings, and render exactly what the compiler emitted
a span of code may be owned by several sentences at once: every span carries all of its owners, and lighting any owner also lights its co-owners wherever ownership is joint.

draw the sentences as a hasse diagram of range containment on the left: a sentence hangs below the one whose range most directly contains its own, covering edges only and never transitive ones, the root on top; a sentence nesting inside several parents gets an edge to each, which the folder tree cannot show
the page keeps the ownership index: which spans a sentence owns, and which sentences own a span. every span listens, so hovering code lights every sentence that owns it in the diagram.

map.why: the html scaffold and the glue between blocks — doctype, head, tag seams, the script wrapper. plumbing the page implies but no sentence describes
the whole look — colours, spacing, light and dark theme. chosen by convention, nobody asked for it
html escaping glue so code renders as text, demanded by the medium not by a sentence
